content:
Overview

AutomatedLab provisioning of a Windows Server 2022 + Active Directory lab on my workstation. Single PowerShell invocation transforms a raw Server 2022 ISO into a running domain controller, member server, and joined workstations on an isolated /24 virtual network. The lab is a sandbox — what it teaches is how an AD environment behaves end-to-end, not production AD operations at scale.

What the Lab Provisions

AutomatedLab's New-LabDefinition with a /24 subnet I defined manually rather than letting the framework auto-pick. The provisioning sequence handles Hyper-V VM creation, Windows Server 2022 installation, AD DS role install, forest promotion, and member server / workstation domain join. The OU structure (IT, HR, Sales) and forest topology come from AutomatedLab's sample configuration, accepted as-is. The Default Domain Controllers GPO and Default Domain Policy apply their out-of-box baselines — password policy, account lockout, Kerberos defaults, audit policy. No custom GPOs authored in this iteration; the work demonstrated is end-to-end provisioning, not GPO authoring.

What This Demonstrates

Running AutomatedLab successfully is non-trivial — Hyper-V configuration, Windows licensing edge cases, forest promotion timing, and DNS bootstrap order all have to line up before a single VM joins the domain. The lab gives me a working AD environment I can reset and redeploy in roughly fifteen minutes, which is the actual workflow value: experimenting with GPO changes, PowerShell user lifecycle automation, or AD query patterns against a deterministic environment that matches what I'd see in a small enterprise. Production-scale AD operations — large directories, multi-domain forests, federation, lifecycle at headcount — I haven't done. This lab is the deliberate scope: hands-on familiarity with AD mechanics on infrastructure I provisioned and can rebuild.
